enrichment.short_description: Open-source installer that bundles Postfix, Dovecot, and webmail into a complete mail server on Linux and BSD.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: iRedMail is an open-source shell script that automates the deployment of a full mail server stack, including Postfix (SMTP), Dovecot (IMAP/POP3), a database backend (OpenLDAP, MySQL, MariaDB, or PostgreSQL), SpamAssassin and ClamAV for antispam/antivirus, and Roundcube or SOGo for webmail.

The project has been maintained since 2007 and supports major Linux distributions (Rocky, AlmaLinux, Debian, Ubuntu, CentOS Stream) as well as FreeBSD and OpenBSD. A commercial Enterprise Edition and iRedAdmin-Pro admin panel are available for organizations wanting one-click upgrades and additional management features.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: System administrators, small businesses, and hobbyists who want to self-host a complete email platform without manually configuring each component of the mail stack separately.

features.title: One-click mail stack installer
features.description: A single installer script sets up Postfix, Dovecot, database backend, antispam, and antivirus together.

features.title: Webmail included
features.description: Ships with Roundcube or SOGo webmail clients out of the box.

features.title: CalDAV/CardDAV via SOGo
features.description: Optional SOGo groupware adds calendar and contact sync alongside email.

features.title: Antispam and antivirus
features.description: Integrates SpamAssassin and ClamAV for filtering incoming mail.

features.title: Flexible backend choice
features.description: Supports OpenLDAP, MySQL, MariaDB, or PostgreSQL as the storage backend.

features.title: ActiveSync support
features.description: Allows mobile devices to sync mail, contacts, and calendars.

features.title: TLS, SPF, DKIM
features.description: Built-in support for transport encryption and email authentication standards.

features.title: iRedAdmin-Pro (paid add-on)
features.description: Optional commercial web admin panel with extended management features.

pros: Free and open source core edition
Saves significant time versus manually configuring Postfix and Dovecot
Actively maintained since 2007
Wide distribution support across Linux and BSD
cons: Requires Linux/BSD system administration skills to install and maintain
Some advanced management features are gated behind the paid iRedAdmin-Pro add-on
Free edition relies on community support only

faqs.question: Is iRedMail free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the open-source edition is free. A commercial Enterprise Edition and iRedAdmin-Pro admin panel are available as paid options.

faqs.question: What operating systems does iRedMail support?
faqs.answer: CentOS Stream, Rocky Linux, AlmaLinux, Debian, Ubuntu, FreeBSD, and OpenBSD.

faqs.question: Does iRedMail include webmail?
faqs.answer: Yes, it installs Roundcube or SOGo webmail by default.

faqs.question: Can I use iRedMail to host email for my own domain?
faqs.answer: Yes, that is its primary purpose: deploying a complete self-hosted mail server for one or more domains.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted business email
use_cases.description: Small organizations wanting full control over their mail server instead of using a hosted provider.

use_cases.title: Personal or family mail server
use_cases.description: Privacy-conscious individuals running their own domain email.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: iRedMail is a free, open-source mail server installer for Linux/BSD bundling Postfix, Dovecot, webmail, antispam, and antivirus.

enrichment.short_description: Modern, all-in-one open-source mail server in Go with built-in SMTP, IMAP, spam filtering, and webmail.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Mox is a self-hosted email server written in Go by developer mjl-, built to consolidate SMTP, IMAP4, spam filtering, and webmail into a single binary rather than assembling separate components like Postfix, Dovecot, and Rspamd. It implements modern email security standards including SPF, DKIM, DMARC, MTA-STS, DANE, and DNSSEC by default.

The project is sponsored by the NLnet Foundation and the European Commission's NGI (Next Generation Internet) program, and emphasizes code quality through extensive automated and fuzz testing against relevant RFCs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Individuals and small teams who want a modern, security-first mail server without manually integrating multiple separate mail components.

features.title: All-in-one binary
features.description: SMTP, IMAP, webmail, and spam filtering ship in a single Go binary.

features.title: Automatic TLS
features.description: Certificates are provisioned automatically via Let's Encrypt.

features.title: Modern email security by default
features.description: Built-in support for SPF, DKIM, DMARC, MTA-STS, DANE, and DNSSEC.

features.title: Spam filtering
features.description: Reputation-based and content-based spam filtering are included out of the box.

features.title: Quickstart setup
features.description: A quickstart tool can get a working mail server running in around 10 minutes.

features.title: Integrated webmail
features.description: Includes a built-in webmail client, no separate install needed.

features.title: Internationalization support
features.description: Handles internationalized email addresses.

pros: Single binary greatly simplifies deployment compared to a multi-component mail stack
Modern security standards (DANE, MTA-STS, DNSSEC) enabled by default
Fast quickstart setup process
MIT licensed and actively maintained
Backed by NLnet and EU NGI program sponsorship
cons: Newer project, less battle-tested than Postfix/Dovecot in large-scale production
Prebuilt binaries mainly target Linux x86-64
Smaller community and ecosystem than legacy mail server tools

faqs.question: Is Mox free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Mox is released under the MIT license and is free and open source.

faqs.question: What protocols does Mox support?
faqs.answer: SMTP and IMAP4 for mail, plus SPF, DKIM, DMARC, MTA-STS, DANE, and DNSSEC for security.

faqs.question: Does Mox include a webmail client?
faqs.answer: Yes, an integrated webmail client is included.

faqs.question: Who maintains Mox?
faqs.answer: It is developed by mjl-, with sponsorship from the NLnet Foundation and the EU NGI program.

use_cases.title: Personal or small-domain email server
use_cases.description: Running your own domain's email without stitching together Postfix, Dovecot, and Rspamd separately.

use_cases.title: Security-focused self-hosting
use_cases.description: Deploying a mail server with DNSSEC, DANE, and MTA-STS support enabled from the start.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Mox is a free, open-source all-in-one mail server written in Go with built-in SMTP, IMAP, spam filtering, and webmail.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source email alias service that hides your real address behind forwarding aliases, with an option to self-host.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: SimpleLogin lets users generate unique email aliases for each website or service they sign up for, forwarding messages to their real inbox while keeping the primary address hidden. Founded in 2020 and later acquired by Proton AG (maker of Proton Mail), SimpleLogin remains fully open source, with its full codebase available on GitHub.

Unlike most alias-forwarding services, SimpleLogin can also be self-hosted on your own server, making it one of the few options that combines a polished hosted product with a genuinely self-hostable open-source core.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Privacy-conscious individuals who want to avoid exposing their real email address to every website, and technical users who prefer to run their own alias infrastructure.

features.title: Email aliases
features.description: Create unique aliases per website; 10 aliases on the free plan, unlimited on Premium.

features.title: Two-way alias replies
features.description: Reply to or send new mail from an alias without revealing your real address.

features.title: Browser extensions
features.description: Extensions for Chrome, Firefox, and Safari to generate aliases on the fly.

features.title: Mobile apps
features.description: Native iOS and Android apps.

features.title: Custom domains
features.description: Bring your own domain to create branded aliases (unlimited on Premium).

features.title: Catch-all aliasing
features.description: Wildcard/catch-all domain support for auto-generating aliases.

features.title: PGP encryption
features.description: Forwarded mail can be encrypted with PGP.

features.title: TOTP and WebAuthn 2FA
features.description: Two-factor authentication options for account security.

features.title: Self-hostable
features.description: Full source code is open source and can be deployed on your own server.

pros: Genuinely open source and self-hostable, not just free-to-use
Generous free plan with 10 aliases and unlimited bandwidth
Backed by Proton's infrastructure and reputation
Strong security options: PGP encryption, TOTP, and WebAuthn
cons: Free plan is limited to 10 aliases and 1 mailbox
Unlimited aliases, custom domains, and PGP require the paid Premium plan
Self-hosting requires technical setup and maintenance

faqs.question: Is SimpleLogin free?
faqs.answer: Yes, a free plan offers 10 aliases and 1 mailbox. Premium unlocks unlimited aliases and custom domains for $36/year or $4/month.

faqs.question: Can I self-host SimpleLogin?
faqs.answer: Yes, its source code is fully open source and it can be self-hosted on your own server.

faqs.question: Who owns SimpleLogin?
faqs.answer: SimpleLogin was acquired by Proton AG, the company behind Proton Mail, though it continues to operate as an open-source project.

faqs.question: Does SimpleLogin support custom domains?
faqs.answer: Yes, unlimited custom domains are included on the Premium plan.

use_cases.title: Alias-based privacy protection
use_cases.description: Signing up for online services without exposing your real email address, and disabling a single alias to stop spam.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted alias infrastructure
use_cases.description: Running your own instance of the aliasing service for full control over data and routing.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: SimpleLogin is an open-source email alias service that hides your real address; free plan available, with self-hosting supported.

enrichment.short_description: Scalable, open-source IMAP/POP3 mail server built on Node.js and MongoDB, designed for API-driven management.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: WildDuck is an open-source IMAP/POP3 email server built with Node.js and MongoDB, maintained by Zone Media. It's designed for horizontal scalability with a stateless, no-single-point-of-failure architecture, targeting deployments with 1,000 or more mailboxes.

WildDuck handles IMAP/POP3 only; it's typically paired with Haraka for inbound SMTP and ZoneMTA for outbound SMTP to form a complete mail stack. Management is designed to happen primarily through a REST API rather than configuration files.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Developers and hosting providers building custom, API-driven email platforms that need to scale horizontally.

features.title: Full IMAP4rev1 support
features.description: Includes IDLE, CONDSTORE, COMPRESS, and QUOTA extensions.

features.title: REST API
features.description: Manage accounts, mailboxes, messages, filters, autoreplies, and DKIM keys entirely through an API.

features.title: MongoDB-based storage
features.description: Uses MongoDB with sharding support for horizontal scale.

features.title: Stateless architecture
features.description: Designed to avoid single points of failure across the cluster.

features.title: TOTP and WebAuthn MFA
features.description: Modern multi-factor authentication options for mailbox accounts.

features.title: PGP encryption support
features.description: Can encrypt stored mail with PGP.

features.title: Unicode support
features.description: Handles internationalized email addresses and folder names.

features.title: Audit logging
features.description: Tracks account and mailbox activity for auditing.

pros: Fully open source under the EUPL 1.2 license
API-first design well suited to automation and custom mail products
Built for horizontal scale across large numbers of mailboxes
Modern authentication options (TOTP, WebAuthn)
cons: Requires MongoDB plus additional components (Haraka, ZoneMTA) for a full mail stack
More operationally complex than an all-in-one mail server
Best suited to teams with Node.js and MongoDB operations experience

faqs.question: Is wildduck free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is released under the EUPL 1.2 open-source license.

faqs.question: Does wildduck handle SMTP?
faqs.answer: No, wildduck handles IMAP/POP3; it's typically paired with Haraka and ZoneMTA for SMTP.

faqs.question: Does wildduck have an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, a REST API is central to how accounts and mailboxes are managed.

faqs.question: Who maintains wildduck?
faqs.answer: Zone Media maintains the project.

use_cases.title: Large-scale hosted email platforms
use_cases.description: Providers needing horizontal scaling across many mailboxes without a single point of failure.

use_cases.title: API-driven mail products
use_cases.description: Building custom applications on top of a mail server via a REST API rather than config files.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: WildDuck is an open-source, API-driven IMAP/POP3 mail server built on Node.js and MongoDB for scalable self-hosted email.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source IMAP email, contacts, and calendar server used in production since 1993.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Cyrus IMAP is a free and open-source mail, contacts, and calendar server originally developed at Carnegie Mellon University, in active development and production use since 1993. It's built for performance and scalability in large deployments.

The server includes CardDAV and CalDAV support alongside IMAP, Sieve scripting for mail filtering, Cyrus SASL for flexible authentication, and JMAP support, and integrates with antivirus and antispam toolkits.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: System administrators and hosting providers running large-scale, high-performance IMAP infrastructure, including universities and enterprises.

features.title: High-performance IMAP server
features.description: Built with speed and efficiency as core design goals.

features.title: CalDAV and CardDAV
features.description: Built-in calendar and contact server alongside IMAP.

features.title: Sieve scripting
features.description: Server-side mail filtering using the Sieve language.

features.title: Cyrus SASL authentication
features.description: Supports a wide range of authentication mechanisms.

features.title: SPF, DKIM, DMARC support
features.description: Standard email authentication protocols for deliverability and security.

features.title: Replication
features.description: Supports replication for high availability deployments.

features.title: JMAP support
features.description: Implements the modern JMAP protocol in addition to IMAP.

features.title: Antivirus/antispam integration
features.description: Works with external antivirus and antispam toolkits.

pros: Extremely mature, with over 30 years in production use
Strong performance and scalability track record for large installations
Free and open source
Supports modern protocols including JMAP alongside CalDAV/CardDAV
cons: Steeper learning curve for configuration than simpler mail servers
Documentation can be dense for newcomers
Best suited to experienced system administrators rather than casual self-hosters

faqs.question: Is Cyrus IMAP free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is free and open source.

faqs.question: How long has Cyrus IMAP been around?
faqs.answer: Since 1993, originally developed at Carnegie Mellon University.

faqs.question: Does Cyrus support calendars and contacts?
faqs.answer: Yes, via built-in CalDAV and CardDAV support.

faqs.question: Does Cyrus support JMAP?
faqs.answer: Yes, JMAP is supported alongside IMAP.

use_cases.title: Large-scale institutional email
use_cases.description: Universities and enterprises needing a proven, high-performance IMAP backend.

use_cases.title: Combined mail, calendar, and contacts server
use_cases.description: Organizations wanting CalDAV/CardDAV alongside IMAP in a single server.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Cyrus IMAP is a free, open-source email, contacts, and calendar server in production use since 1993, built for scale.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source gateway that lets standard mail and calendar clients connect to Exchange or Exchange Online.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: DavMail is a Java-based gateway that translates standard email and calendar protocols, including POP, IMAP, SMTP, CalDAV, CardDAV, and LDAP, into calls compatible with on-premise Exchange or Exchange Online. This lets users connect regular clients like Thunderbird or Apple Mail/iCal without relying on Outlook.
The application is cross-platform Java software, tested on Windows, Linux (Ubuntu and Fedora), and macOS, and can run headless on a server for remote or mobile clients.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Users on non-Outlook platforms, particularly Linux and macOS, who need to access a corporate Exchange mailbox using standard mail and calendar apps.

features.title: POP and IMAP gateway
features.description: Receive Exchange mail through standard POP or IMAP clients.

features.title: SMTP gateway
features.description: Send mail through Exchange using standard SMTP.

features.title: CalDAV support
features.description: Calendar access including free/busy lookups.

features.title: CardDAV support
features.description: Sync personal contacts with standard CardDAV clients.

features.title: LDAP gateway
features.description: Search the Exchange global address book via LDAP.

features.title: Cross-platform
features.description: Runs on Windows, Linux, and macOS via the JVM.

features.title: Exchange Online support
features.description: Works with both on-premise Exchange and Exchange Online (Microsoft 365).

pros: Free and open source
Enables non-Outlook clients like Thunderbird and Apple Mail/iCal to use Exchange
Actively maintained with recent releases
Works across Windows, Linux, and macOS
cons: Requires running an extra local or server-side gateway process
Java-based, so a JVM is required
Initial configuration can be fiddly for less technical users

faqs.question: Is DavMail free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is open source.

faqs.question: Does DavMail work with Microsoft 365 (Exchange Online)?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports both on-premise Exchange and Exchange Online.

faqs.question: What email clients work with DavMail?
faqs.answer: Any standard POP/IMAP/SMTP/CalDAV/CardDAV client, such as Thunderbird or Apple Mail/iCal.

faqs.question: What platforms does DavMail run on?
faqs.answer: Windows, Linux, and macOS; it can also run headless on a server.

use_cases.title: Non-Outlook access to Exchange
use_cases.description: Employees who prefer Thunderbird, Apple Mail, or other clients but need Exchange connectivity.

use_cases.title: Cross-platform Exchange calendar sync
use_cases.description: Accessing Exchange calendars via CalDAV from non-Windows systems.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: DavMail is a free, open-source gateway that lets Thunderbird, Apple Mail, and other clients connect to Exchange via POP/IMAP/CalDAV.

enrichment.short_description: Widely used, high-performance open-source IMAP and POP3 server, with an optional commercial Dovecot Pro edition.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Dovecot is one of the most widely deployed open-source IMAP and POP3 servers, known for performance, standards compliance, and security. The open-source core is free, while Dovecot Pro is a separate commercial, enterprise-grade edition aimed at telcos, ISPs, and large hosting providers that need dynamic scalability and dedicated support.
The dovecot.org website and project are maintained by Open-Xchange GmbH. Dovecot handles IMAP/POP3 only and does not send mail itself; it's commonly paired with a mail transfer agent such as Postfix.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: System administrators of all sizes, from individual self-hosters to large hosting providers, who need a reliable IMAP/POP3 backend.

features.title: High-performance IMAP/POP3
features.description: Optimized for speed and standards compliance.

features.title: mbox and Maildir support
features.description: Works with both mailbox storage formats.

features.title: Flexible authentication
features.description: Supports many different authentication databases and mechanisms.

features.title: Self-optimizing indexes
features.description: Includes self-healing index behavior for reliability.

features.title: Clustering and NFS support
features.description: Built for deployment across clustered and networked storage environments.

features.title: Plugin extensibility
features.description: Supports plugins and Lua scripting for customization.

features.title: Migration tools
features.description: Includes tools to migrate from existing IMAP/POP3 servers.

features.title: Dovecot Pro
features.description: Paid enterprise edition with dynamic scalability and dedicated support for telcos, ISPs, and hosting providers.

pros: Extremely mature and widely deployed across the industry
Strong performance and standards compliance
Free, open-source core
Active security disclosure program
cons: Advanced enterprise scaling features are gated behind the paid Dovecot Pro edition
Configuration can be complex for large deployments
Does not handle SMTP itself; requires pairing with an MTA like Postfix

faqs.question: Is Dovecot free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core Dovecot software is free and open source; Dovecot Pro is a separate paid enterprise edition.

faqs.question: Does Dovecot handle sending mail?
faqs.answer: No, Dovecot is an IMAP/POP3 server; it is commonly paired with a mail transfer agent like Postfix for SMTP.

faqs.question: Who maintains Dovecot?
faqs.answer: The dovecot.org website is maintained by Open-Xchange GmbH.

faqs.question: What mailbox formats does Dovecot support?
faqs.answer: Both mbox and Maildir formats.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted mail server backend
use_cases.description: Pairing Dovecot with Postfix for a complete self-hosted mail stack.

use_cases.title: Large-scale hosting or ISP email
use_cases.description: Using Dovecot Pro's clustering and support for high-volume IMAP/POP3 deployments.

features: Built for telcos, ISPs, and hosting providers

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Dovecot is a widely used, high-performance open-source IMAP/POP3 server, with a paid Dovecot Pro edition for large-scale hosting.

enrichment.short_description: Lightweight, easy-to-configure open-source SMTP server written in Go, built to avoid common misconfigurations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: chasquid is an SMTP server (mail transfer agent) written in Go by developer Alberto Bertogli, offered as an easier-to-configure alternative to Postfix or Exim for individuals and small setups. It's designed so that secure configuration is the default, preventing common issues such as accidental open relays.
The project supports multiple virtual domains, user aliases, internationalized domain names, and automatic TLS certificate management via Let's Encrypt, along with SPF and MTA-STS verification.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Individuals and small organizations who want to run their own domain's SMTP server without dealing with the complexity of Postfix or Exim.

features.title: Simple configuration
features.description: Designed to be easy to set up correctly compared to traditional MTAs.

features.title: Multiple virtual domains
features.description: Supports hosting several domains and user aliases from one server.

features.title: Internationalized domain names
features.description: Supports IDNA for non-ASCII domain names.

features.title: Automatic TLS
features.description: Integrates with Let's Encrypt for certificate management.

features.title: SPF and MTA-STS verification
features.description: Built-in checks to improve deliverability and security.

features.title: Secure-by-default design
features.description: Prevents insecure configurations such as open relays.

features.title: Single static binary
features.description: Written in Go, simplifying deployment.

pros: Simpler to configure than Postfix or Exim
Secure defaults reduce the risk of misconfiguration
Free and open source under Apache 2.0
Built-in Let's Encrypt integration
cons: Smaller community than Postfix or Exim
Fewer plugins and extensions than more mature MTAs
Best suited to small-scale rather than large enterprise deployments

faqs.question: Is chasquid free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is released under the Apache License 2.0.

faqs.question: Who develops chasquid?
faqs.answer: It is created and maintained by Alberto Bertogli.

faqs.question: What language is chasquid written in?
faqs.answer: Go.

faqs.question: Does chasquid support multiple domains?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports multiple virtual domains and user aliases.

use_cases.title: Personal domain email server
use_cases.description: Individuals wanting a simple, secure SMTP server for their own domain.

use_cases.title: Small business mail relay
use_cases.description: Small teams needing straightforward SMTP without Postfix's configuration overhead.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: chasquid is a free, open-source SMTP server written in Go, designed for easy, secure configuration on small mail setups.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source integrated mail and groupware server combining ESMTP, IMAP, POP3, webmail, and calendaring.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Courier (Courier MTA) is an open-source, integrated mail and groupware server released under the GNU General Public License. It bundles ESMTP, IMAP, POP3, webmail, and calendar/scheduling services into a single framework rather than requiring separate components.

It stores mail natively in maildir format, with legacy mbox support, and can be configured as a mail relay, a domain-specific server, or a hybrid setup. It compiles on most POSIX-based systems, including Linux, BSD, Solaris, and AIX.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: System administrators who want one integrated package rather than assembling separate MTA, IMAP, and webmail components.

features.title: Integrated protocol support
features.description: ESMTP, IMAP, and POP3 in a single server framework.

features.title: Maildir storage
features.description: Native maildir format storage, with legacy mbox support.

features.title: Webmail and admin UI
features.description: Includes web-based webmail access and administration tools.

features.title: TLS/SSL and SPF
features.description: Supports encrypted connections and Sender Policy Framework validation.

features.title: Authenticated SMTP relay
features.description: Supports authenticated relay configurations.

features.title: Mailing list management
features.description: Built-in tools for running mailing lists.

features.title: Mail filtering
features.description: Includes message filtering capabilities.

features.title: IMAP/POP3 proxy aggregation
features.description: Can aggregate multiple backend mail servers behind a single proxy.

pros: All-in-one mail and groupware stack in a single project
Free and open source under the GPL
Runs on most POSIX systems including Linux, BSD, Solaris, and AIX
Mature, long-standing project
cons: Development pace has slowed compared to Postfix and Dovecot
Smaller community and fewer recent updates
Documentation and interface feel dated

faqs.question: Is Courier MTA free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is licensed under the GNU General Public License.

faqs.question: What mailbox format does Courier use?
faqs.answer: Maildir natively, with legacy mbox support.

faqs.question: What operating systems does Courier support?
faqs.answer: Most POSIX-based systems, including Linux, BSD, Solaris, and AIX.

faqs.question: Does Courier include webmail?
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes web-based webmail and administration tools.

use_cases.title: All-in-one self-hosted mail server
use_cases.description: Teams wanting a single integrated package instead of combining separate MTA, IMAP, and webmail tools.

use_cases.title: Mailing list hosting
use_cases.description: Organizations needing built-in mailing list management alongside regular email.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Courier MTA is a free, open-source integrated mail server combining ESMTP, IMAP, POP3, webmail, and calendaring in one package.

enrichment.short_description: Lightweight, open-source store-and-forward SMTP server with POP access, built for simple mail relaying.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: E-MailRelay (EmailRelay) is a lightweight, single-process SMTP store-and-forward mail server with POP access to spooled messages, released under the permissive FSFAP open-source license. It uses non-blocking I/O for scalability, with an architecture similar to servers like Squid and nginx.

It supports smarthost forwarding or DNS MX-based routing, integrates with SpamAssassin, and can block connections via DNSBLs. Its policy-free design allows custom handling to be implemented through external scripts.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Developers and system administrators who need a simple, low-dependency relay or forwarding server rather than a full mail stack.

features.title: Single-process architecture
features.description: Uses non-blocking I/O for efficient scalability.

features.title: Smarthost or MX-based routing
features.description: Can forward via a fixed smarthost or standard DNS MX lookups.

features.title: SpamAssassin integration
features.description: Can route messages through SpamAssassin for spam scoring.

features.title: DNSBL blocking
features.description: Blocks connections from IPs listed in DNS-based blocklists.

features.title: Address verification
features.description: Supports verifying recipient addresses before accepting mail.

features.title: SMTP and POP support
features.description: Provides SMTP for relaying and POP access to spooled messages, with maildir-based IMAP integration.

features.title: Policy-free scripting hooks
features.description: Custom handling can be implemented via external scripts.

features.title: Cross-platform
features.description: Runs on Unix-like systems and Windows, including as a Windows Service.

pros: Minimal dependencies and easy to install
Free and open source under the permissive FSFAP license
Cross-platform, running on both Linux and Windows
Lightweight and efficient for simple relay use cases
cons: Not a full-featured mail server; no built-in IMAP server, only maildir/POP access
Smaller community than major mail transfer agents
Best suited to relay and store-and-forward roles rather than full mailbox hosting

faqs.question: Is EmailRelay free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is released under the permissive FSFAP license.

faqs.question: What does EmailRelay do?
faqs.answer: It is a store-and-forward SMTP server with POP access to spooled messages, commonly used as a simple mail relay.

faqs.question: Does EmailRelay run on Windows?
faqs.answer: Yes, including as a Windows Service.

faqs.question: Does EmailRelay include spam filtering?
faqs.answer: It integrates with SpamAssassin and supports DNSBL-based connection blocking.

use_cases.title: Simple SMTP relay
use_cases.description: Applications or servers that need to send outbound mail without running a full mail server.

use_cases.title: Store-and-forward mail spooling
use_cases.description: Environments needing lightweight message queuing with POP retrieval.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: E-MailRelay is a free, open-source, lightweight SMTP store-and-forward server with POP access, for simple mail relaying.
